title: The Editor, the Scene Tree, and Your First Moving Sprite
html:
Welcome to your engine

You finished the engine-agnostic Foundations and you picked Godot 4 with GDScript as your single path. Good choice. This track stands entirely on its own: by the end you will not have read a single line about another engine, and you will have shipped a portfolio of games across many genres. Here is the arc you are about to build, all in Godot: an interactive scene, Pong, Breakout, a 2D platformer, a top-down adventure/shooter, a wave-survival game, a puzzle game, an endless runner, and finally a polished capstone you can put in front of an employer or on itch.io.

Every lesson ends with a Build This game, and those games connect into one continuous journey. Let us start by understanding the engine itself.

Installing and opening Godot

Download Godot 4 from the official site. It is a single executable with no installer required — that small download is the entire engine. Launch it and you land on the Project Manager. Click New, name it HelloGodot, choose the Compatibility renderer (it exports to web cleanly, which matters later), and open it.

The four docks

The editor has a consistent layout you will use for the whole track:

DockWhat it does
SceneThe tree of nodes in the currently open scene
FileSystemYour project's files on disk (scenes, scripts, images)
InspectorProperties of the selected node
ViewportThe center — your 2D/3D editing canvas

Nodes and the scene tree

Everything in Godot is a node: a small object that does one job. A Sprite2D draws an image. A Label shows text. You arrange nodes into a tree, and a tree saved to disk is a scene (.tscn). A whole game is just scenes nesting inside scenes.

Create a root node: in the Scene dock click + and add a Node2D. Rename it Main. Now add a child: select Main, click +, add a Sprite2D. In the Inspector, drag Godot's built-in icon.svg from FileSystem into the Sprite's Texture slot. Save the scene as main.tscn with Ctrl/Cmd+S.

Tip.
Press F6 to run the current scene, or F5 to run the main scene of the whole project. Godot will ask you to pick a main scene the first time you press F5 — choose main.tscn.

Your first script

A node becomes interactive when you attach a script. Select the Sprite2D, click the scroll-with-plus icon at the top of the Scene dock, keep the language as GDScript, and create sprite_2d.gd. You get a starter file with two functions Godot calls for you: _ready() runs once when the node enters the tree, and _process(delta) runs every frame, where delta is the seconds since the last frame.

sprite_2d.gd
# extends tells Godot which node type this script powers extends Sprite2D @export var speed: float = 200.0 func _process(delta: float) -> void: # Vector2 is an (x, y) pair; we build a direction from input var dir := Vector2.ZERO if Input.is_key_pressed(KEY_RIGHT): dir.x += 1 if Input.is_key_pressed(KEY_LEFT): dir.x -= 1 if Input.is_key_pressed(KEY_DOWN): dir.y += 1 if Input.is_key_pressed(KEY_UP): dir.y -= 1 position += dir.normalized() * speed * delta

The @export keyword exposes speed in the Inspector so you can tune it without editing code. Multiplying by delta makes movement frame-rate independent — a fast PC and a slow PC move the sprite the same real-world distance per second. normalized() keeps diagonal movement from being faster than straight movement.

Press F6. Your sprite glides around with the arrow keys. That is the whole engine loop in miniature: a tree of nodes, scripts reacting each frame, properties you can tune live.

Lab.
Add a second @export var for a color tint and apply it in _ready() with modulate = Color(1, 0.5, 0.5). Change the exported speed in the Inspector and re-run to feel the difference.
🎮 Build This: Interactive Scene — Roam

This starts the arc with your very first interactive scene (no genre yet, just movement) — the seed everything else grows from.

  1. Make a fresh scene with a Node2D root named Roam.
  2. Add a Sprite2D child with the Godot icon texture.
  3. Attach the movement script above and expose speed.
  4. Add a second Sprite2D as an obstacle and tint it with modulate.
  5. Set roam.tscn as the main scene and run with F5. Confirm smooth, frame-rate-independent movement in all directions.

title: Nodes, Node Types, and the GDScript Language
html:
GDScript in one lesson

GDScript is Godot's own language: Python-like, indentation-based, and tightly fused with the node system. You already wrote some. Now let us make it solid because every game ahead depends on it.

Variables and typing

Declare with var, constants with const. Types are optional but recommended — they catch bugs and speed up code. Use := to infer a type from the value, or : Type to state it.

basics.gd
extends Node const MAX_HP := 100 # inferred int, constant var hp: int = MAX_HP var player_name := "Avara" # inferred String var alive := true # inferred bool var inventory: Array[String] = [] # typed array

Functions, conditionals, loops

Functions use func name(args) -> ReturnType:. Indentation defines blocks. Loops are for and while.

logic.gd
func take_damage(amount: int) -> void: hp -= amount if hp <= 0: hp = 0 alive = false print("You died") elif hp < 20: print("Low health!") func heal_all() -> void: for i in range(3): hp = min(hp + 10, MAX_HP)

The node toolbox

You will reach for the same handful of nodes constantly. Learn what each is for:

NodeRole
Node2DBase for anything with a 2D position/rotation/scale
Sprite2DDraws a single image
AnimatedSprite2DDraws frame-based animations
CharacterBody2DA body you move with code (players, enemies)
Area2DDetects overlaps (pickups, triggers, hitboxes)
CollisionShape2DDefines the physical shape of a body/area
Label / ButtonUI (a Control node)
TimerFires after a delay or repeatedly

Finding nodes from script

A script often needs to reach a sibling or child node. The cleanest way is @onready plus the $ shorthand or % unique-name access.

access.gd
extends Node2D # $Name walks the child path; @onready waits until the tree is built @onready var label: Label = $ScoreLabel @onready var timer: Timer = $Spawn/Timer func _ready() -> void: label.text = "Score: 0"
Watch out.
Using $Child in a variable's initializer at the top of the file will crash, because children do not exist yet when the script object is created. That is exactly why @onready exists — it defers the lookup until _ready().

Classes and instances

Each script implicitly defines a class. Add class_name Enemy at the top and it becomes a type you can reference everywhere and even add from the node-creation dialog. This is how you will build reusable game objects.

Tip.
Run with the Output and Debugger panels open at the bottom. print() goes to Output; runtime errors with a full stack trace go to Debugger. Reading stack traces is the single fastest way to improve.
🎮 Build This: Interactive Scene — Roam v2 (Collectibles)

This extends Roam from Lesson 1 — still pre-genre groundwork, now adding state and logic with GDScript.

  1. Open roam.tscn and attach a fresh script to the root with class_name RoamGame.
  2. Add a Label child named ScoreLabel; reference it with @onready.
  3. Add a var score: int = 0 and a function add_point() that increments it and updates the label text.
  4. For now, call add_point() when the player presses the Space key (use Input.is_action_just_pressed next lesson; for today Input.is_key_pressed(KEY_SPACE) with a cooldown works).
  5. Run and confirm the score label updates. You now have movement plus game state — the foundation for Pong.

title: Signals, Input Mapping, and Building PONG
html:
Signals: how nodes talk

Polling input every frame works, but Godot's real superpower is signals — an event system where one node emits a message and any number of others react, without the emitter knowing who is listening. A button emits pressed. A timer emits timeout. An Area2D emits body_entered. This keeps your code decoupled.

Connecting signals

You can connect in the editor (Node dock > Signals tab, double-click a signal, pick a target) or in code. In code is explicit and version-control-friendly:

signals.gd
extends Node @onready var timer: Timer = $Timer func _ready() -> void: # connect Timer.timeout to our handler function timer.timeout.connect(_on_timer_timeout) timer.start() func _on_timer_timeout() -> void: print("tick")

Defining your own signals

Declare a custom signal with signal and fire it with emit(). This is how a ball tells the game it scored.

ball.gd
extends Area2D signal scored(side: String) func _on_left_goal() -> void: scored.emit("right") # right player gets the point

Input mapping

Hard-coding KEY_UP is fragile. Instead define named actions in Project > Project Settings > Input Map. Add actions like p1_up, p1_down, p2_up, p2_down and bind keys (and gamepad buttons) to each. Then read them by name:

input.gd
# returns -1..1 from a pair of actions, gamepad-aware var dir := Input.get_axis("p1_up", "p1_down") # one-shot events (jump, fire) use just_pressed if Input.is_action_just_pressed("ui_accept"): print("start")
Rule.
Never reference raw key codes in gameplay scripts. Map an action, read the action. Your future self (and your gamepad-using players) will thank you.

Pong's anatomy

Pong is the perfect first real game: two paddles, one ball, a score. Paddles are simple bodies clamped to the screen. The ball moves at a constant velocity and bounces. Two Area2D goals on the left and right edges detect the ball and emit a score.

paddle.gd
extends Node2D @export var up_action := "p1_up" @export var down_action := "p1_down" @export var speed := 400.0 func _process(delta: float) -> void: var v := Input.get_axis(up_action, down_action) position.y += v * speed * delta position.y = clamp(position.y, 40, 608)
🎮 Build This: Arcade — PONG

This starts the ARCADE genre — your first complete game. It introduces the paddle-and-ball collision you will reuse in Breakout next lesson.

  1. New scene pong.tscn, root Node2D. Add two paddle Node2Ds (left/right) with the script above; set their actions to p1_* and p2_*.
  2. Define those four actions plus ui_accept in the Input Map.
  3. Add a ball as an Area2D with a CollisionShape2D; give it a velocity: Vector2 and move it each frame. Reflect velocity.x when it overlaps a paddle, reflect velocity.y at top/bottom.
  4. Add left/right goal Area2Ds. Emit a custom scored(side) signal; the main script listens, increments a Label score, and resets the ball to center.
  5. Wire start/restart to ui_accept. Run with two players and play a full match.

title: Sprites, AnimatedSprite2D, Audio, and BREAKOUT
html:
From static images to animation

A Sprite2D shows one image. For frames that change over time — a flickering coin, a walking character — use AnimatedSprite2D, which holds a SpriteFrames resource: a set of named animations, each a list of frames with a playback speed.

Building a SpriteFrames resource

  1. Add an AnimatedSprite2D node.
  2. In the Inspector, click the Sprite Frames property and choose New SpriteFrames.
  3. Click it to open the SpriteFrames editor at the bottom. Create animations like idle and spin.
  4. Drag image frames in, set FPS, and toggle Loop.
coin.gd
extends AnimatedSprite2D func _ready() -> void: play("spin") # start an animation by name func pop() -> void: play("collect") await animation_finished # pause until it ends queue_free() # remove this node

await pauses a function until a signal fires — here we wait for the collect animation, then delete the node. queue_free() is the safe way to destroy a node at the end of the frame.

Sprite sheets

If your art is one image with many frames in a grid, set AnimatedSprite2D to use a sheet, or use a plain Sprite2D with Hframes/Vframes and the frame property. For tile-based art later you will slice sheets automatically.

Audio

Two nodes cover almost everything: AudioStreamPlayer for non-positional sound (music, UI clicks) and AudioStreamPlayer2D for sound that pans/attenuates by position. Drag a .wav or .ogg into the Stream slot and call play().

sfx.gd
extends Node @onready var bounce: AudioStreamPlayer = $Bounce @onready var music: AudioStreamPlayer = $Music func _ready() -> void: music.play() func on_hit() -> void: bounce.pitch_scale = randf_range(0.9, 1.1) # vary pitch so it feels alive bounce.play()
Tip.
Use Audio Buses (the Audio panel at the bottom) to group sounds: a Music bus and an SFX bus you can mute or volume-control independently. Your settings menu in the capstone will adjust these.

Reusing Pong's collision in Breakout

Breakout is Pong with one paddle, a wall of bricks, and a ball that destroys what it touches. The paddle and ball-bounce logic transfer almost directly — you are extending the arcade genre. The new idea is instancing many objects: build one brick.tscn, then spawn a grid of them.

brick_grid.gd
extends Node2D @export var brick_scene: PackedScene @export var cols := 10 @export var rows := 5 func _ready() -> void: for r in range(rows): for c in range(cols): var b := brick_scene.instantiate() b.position = Vector2(80 + c * 96, 80 + r * 40) add_child(b)
🎮 Build This: Arcade — BREAKOUT

This extends the ARCADE genre from Pong, reusing your paddle and ball-bounce code while adding instancing, animation, and audio.

  1. New scene breakout.tscn. Copy your Pong paddle and ball logic; keep only one paddle at the bottom that moves left/right.
  2. Make brick.tscn as an Area2D with an AnimatedSprite2D; on overlap with the ball play a break animation then queue_free().
  3. Spawn a grid using brick_grid.gd and an exported brick_scene.
  4. Add a bounce SFX (vary pitch) and looping music via audio buses.
  5. Track score on a Label; when all bricks are gone, show a win and let ui_accept restart. Lose the ball off the bottom = game over.

title: 2D Physics, Tilemaps, and a 2D PLATFORMER
html:
Real physics bodies

So far you moved nodes by hand. Now meet Godot's 2D physics. Three body types matter:

BodyUse it for
StaticBody2DWalls, ground — never moves
CharacterBody2DPlayer/enemies you move with code but want collision response
RigidBody2DObjects fully simulated by the physics engine

For platformers and most action games you want CharacterBody2D: you control it, but it slides along walls and stops at floors automatically via move_and_slide().

Gravity and jumping

player.gd
extends CharacterBody2D @export var speed := 300.0 @export var jump_force := -600.0 # negative = up @export var gravity := 1500.0 func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: # apply gravity while airborne if not is_on_floor(): velocity.y += gravity * delta # jump only when grounded if Input.is_action_just_pressed("jump") and is_on_floor(): velocity.y = jump_force # horizontal movement var dir := Input.get_axis("move_left", "move_right") velocity.x = dir * speed move_and_slide()

Use _physics_process (fixed timestep) for physics, not _process. move_and_slide() reads velocity, moves the body, resolves collisions, and updates is_on_floor() for next frame.

Collision layers and masks

Every body has a layer (what it is) and a mask (what it scans for). A coin sits on the pickups layer; the player's mask includes pickups so it detects them, while enemies ignore them. Name your layers in Project Settings > Layer Names > 2D Physics so the checkboxes read like Player, World, Enemy, Pickup.

Rule.
Two objects only interact if one's mask includes the other's layer. Most collision bugs are layer/mask mistakes — check these first.

Tilemaps

Hand-placing platforms is painful. A TileMapLayer lets you paint a level from a TileSet — a palette of tiles, each able to carry its own collision shape. Workflow:

  1. Add a TileMapLayer node.
  2. Create a TileSet in its Tile Set property; add your tile sheet as an atlas and auto-slice it.
  3. In the TileSet editor, give solid tiles a physics layer and draw collision polygons on them.
  4. Use the TileMap bottom panel to paint your level.

Now your painted ground is solid automatically — no per-platform StaticBody2D needed. This is how you build whole worlds fast.

Area2D for pickups and hazards

Use an Area2D (not a body) for coins, spikes, and checkpoints. Connect its body_entered signal and check what entered:

coin.gd
extends Area2D signal collected func _ready() -> void: body_entered.connect(_on_body_entered) func _on_body_entered(body: Node2D) -> void: if body.is_in_group("player"): collected.emit() queue_free()
Tip.
Add nodes to groups (Node dock > Groups, e.g. player) so detection code asks "are you a player?" instead of checking exact class paths. Groups keep gameplay code flexible.
🎮 Build This: Platformer — Leap

This STARTS A NEW GENRE: the 2D platformer — your first game with gravity, jumping, and tile-painted levels.

  1. New scene leap.tscn. Add a CharacterBody2D player with the gravity/jump script and an AnimatedSprite2D (idle/run/jump animations).
  2. Define move_left, move_right, and jump actions.
  3. Add a TileMapLayer, build a TileSet with collision, and paint a level with gaps and platforms.
  4. Set layers/masks: player on Player, ground on World, coins on Pickup.
  5. Scatter coin.tscn Area2Ds and add a spike hazard that resets the player to a start position. Track coins on a Label. Reach a goal flag to win.

title: Top-Down Movement, Aiming, and Shooting Projectiles
html:
A different kind of character

A platformer pulls you down with gravity. A top-down game has no gravity — the player moves freely in eight directions and usually aims independently of movement. The body is still a CharacterBody2D, but the movement code changes.

topdown_player.gd
extends CharacterBody2D @export var speed := 260.0 @export var bullet_scene: PackedScene @onready var muzzle: Marker2D = $Muzzle func _physics_process(_delta: float) -> void: var dir := Input.get_vector("move_left", "move_right", "move_up", "move_down") velocity = dir * speed move_and_slide() # face the mouse look_at(get_global_mouse_position()) if Input.is_action_just_pressed("fire"): shoot()

Input.get_vector(...) returns a normalized Vector2 from four actions in one call — perfect for top-down. look_at() rotates the node toward a point; get_global_mouse_position() gives the cursor in world space.

Spawning projectiles

A bullet is its own scene you instantiate at the muzzle and add to the world. Crucially, add it to the scene tree, not as a child of the player — otherwise it moves and rotates with the player.

shoot.gd
func shoot() -> void: var b := bullet_scene.instantiate() b.global_position = muzzle.global_position b.rotation = rotation get_tree().current_scene.add_child(b)
bullet.gd
extends Area2D @export var speed := 700.0 @export var damage := 1 func _ready() -> void: body_entered.connect(_on_hit) $Lifetime.timeout.connect(queue_free) # Timer auto-deletes stray bullets func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: position += Vector2.RIGHT.rotated(rotation) * speed * delta func _on_hit(body: Node2D) -> void: if body.has_method("take_damage"): body.take_damage(damage) queue_free()
Watch out.
Always give bullets a lifetime Timer. A bullet that flies off-screen forever is a memory leak; thousands of them tank performance. The Timer's timeout calls queue_free().

Health and the duck-typed interface

Notice body.has_method("take_damage"). Instead of checking exact types, we ask "can you take damage?" Any node — enemy, breakable crate, the player — that defines take_damage(amount) works with every bullet. This loose coupling scales beautifully as you add content.

enemy.gd
extends CharacterBody2D class_name Enemy @export var hp := 3 func take_damage(amount: int) -> void: hp -= amount if hp <= 0: queue_free()
Tip.
Use a Marker2D as a muzzle so you can position the spawn point visually and even animate it. Markers are invisible at runtime and exist purely to mark a spot.
🎮 Build This: Top-Down Shooter — Outpost

This STARTS A NEW GENRE: the top-down adventure/shooter. The player you build here is reused for the wave-survival game next lesson.

  1. New scene outpost.tscn. Add the CharacterBody2D top-down player with eight-direction movement, mouse aiming, and a Muzzle marker.
  2. Define move_up/down/left/right and fire actions.
  3. Make bullet.tscn (Area2D + lifetime Timer) and wire shooting on the fire action.
  4. Make enemy.tscn with take_damage() and simple chase behavior (move toward the player each frame).
  5. Use collision layers: bullets hit Enemy, enemies hit Player. Add walls with a TileMapLayer and give the player health. Display HP and a kill count on Labels.

title: Spawners, Timers, Difficulty Curves, and WAVE SURVIVAL
html:
Procedural spawning

A wave-survival game is a stress test of everything so far: your top-down player faces endless, escalating enemy waves. The new craft is controlled randomness and pacing — spawning enemies over time, at growing intensity, in a way that feels fair.

A spawner with a Timer

spawner.gd
extends Node2D @export var enemy_scene: PackedScene @export var spawn_radius := 600.0 @onready var timer: Timer = $Timer var wave := 1 func _ready() -> void: timer.timeout.connect(_spawn) timer.start() func _spawn() -> void: var e := enemy_scene.instantiate() # pick a random angle on a circle around the center var angle := randf() * TAU e.global_position = global_position + Vector2.RIGHT.rotated(angle) * spawn_radius get_tree().current_scene.add_child(e)

randf() gives a float in 0..1; TAU is a full circle in radians. Multiplying gives a uniformly random direction so enemies pour in from all sides.

Difficulty curve

A survival game lives or dies on pacing. Tie spawn rate and enemy stats to a rising wave counter. A second Timer advances the wave every N seconds and shortens the spawn interval:

difficulty.gd
func next_wave() -> void: wave += 1 # spawn faster, but clamp so it never gets impossible timer.wait_time = max(0.25, 1.5 - wave * 0.1) print("Wave " + str(wave))
Tip.
Always clamp or max your difficulty values. An unbounded curve spawns thousands of enemies and freezes the game. Bound everything: spawn rate, enemy count, projectile count.

Object pooling for performance

Constantly instancing and freeing enemies/bullets can stutter. For heavy spawning, keep a pool of reusable nodes: hide and deactivate instead of queue_free(), then reactivate from the pool. Start simple (instantiate/free) and only pool if you see frame drops in the profiler (Debugger > Profiler).

An Autoload for global game state

Score, wave, and game-over state need to live somewhere persistent. Create a singleton with Project Settings > Autoload: add game_state.gd with a node name like GameState. Now any script reads GameState.score directly.

game_state.gd
extends Node signal score_changed(value: int) var score := 0 func add_score(n: int) -> void: score += n score_changed.emit(score) # UI listens and updates
Rule.
Autoloads are global state. Keep them small and focused (one for game state, maybe one for audio). Do not dump everything into one giant singleton — that becomes a maintenance nightmare.
🎮 Build This: Wave Survival — Last Stand

This STARTS A NEW GENRE: wave survival, and extends your top-down player and enemy from Lesson 6.

  1. New scene last_stand.tscn. Drop in your top-down player and bullet from Outpost.
  2. Add a Spawner that rings enemies in around the arena using the radius/angle trick.
  3. Add an Autoload GameState tracking score and connect score_changed to a Label.
  4. Increase difficulty each wave by shortening the spawn interval (clamped) and nudging enemy speed.
  5. On player death, show "You survived N waves" and let ui_accept restart. Add SFX for shots/hits and looping tense music.

title: Grid Logic, UI (Control), and Two New Genres: PUZZLE & RUNNER
html:
Thinking in grids

Puzzle games like Sokoban live on a grid, not in free space. The trick is to separate the logic grid (a 2D array of cell states) from the visual nodes. You compute moves on the array, then move sprites to match. A grid cell maps to a pixel position by multiplying by tile size.

sokoban.gd
extends Node2D const TILE := 64 var player_cell := Vector2i(2, 2) var walls := {} # Dictionary used as a set of Vector2i var boxes := {} # cell -> box node func try_move(step: Vector2i) -> void: var target := player_cell + step if walls.has(target): return if boxes.has(target): var beyond := target + step if walls.has(beyond) or boxes.has(beyond): return push_box(target, beyond) player_cell = target $Player.position = Vector2(player_cell) * TILE

Reading input as discrete steps (not continuous velocity) is what makes it a puzzle: each press is one grid move. Use is_action_just_pressed for crisp, one-step-per-tap control.

UI with Control nodes

Gameplay uses Node2D; interface uses Control. Control nodes have anchors and containers that lay themselves out responsively across screen sizes. Key nodes:

NodeUse
ControlBase UI element with anchoring
Label / ButtonText and clickable buttons
VBoxContainer/HBoxContainerAuto-stack children vertically/horizontally
CanvasLayerDraws UI on top of the world, ignoring camera
menu.gd
extends Control func _ready() -> void: $VBox/PlayButton.pressed.connect(_on_play) $VBox/QuitButton.pressed.connect(get_tree().quit) func _on_play() -> void: get_tree().change_scene_to_file("res://runner.tscn")

change_scene_to_file swaps the whole running scene — that is your menu-to-game flow.

Endless runner: procedural generation

A runner scrolls the world toward a fixed-position player who only jumps. The world is generated on the fly: spawn ground/obstacle chunks ahead, recycle them once off-screen. The player's run is faked by moving the world left.

runner.gd
extends Node2D @export var scroll_speed := 400.0 @export var obstacle_scene: PackedScene var spawn_x := 1200.0 func _process(delta: float) -> void: for ob in get_tree().get_nodes_in_group("scroll"): ob.position.x -= scroll_speed * delta if ob.position.x < -100: ob.queue_free() func spawn_obstacle() -> void: var o := obstacle_scene.instantiate() o.add_to_group("scroll") o.position = Vector2(spawn_x, randi_range(500, 620)) add_child(o)
Tip.
For an infinite floor, place two ground tiles side by side; when one scrolls fully off the left, snap it back to the right of the other. This "leapfrog" trick gives a seamless treadmill cheaply.
🎮 Build This: TWO games — Puzzle (Shift) and Runner (Dash)

This STARTS TWO NEW GENRES. First a puzzle (Sokoban-style), then an endless runner (procedural) — broadening your portfolio to seven distinct games.

  1. Shift (puzzle): grid-based Sokoban using the logic-array approach; push boxes onto goal tiles, win when all are covered. Add a move counter and reset key.
  2. Dash (runner): a fixed-position jumper (reuse your platformer gravity/jump) over scrolling, recycled obstacles; distance score increases over time and speed ramps up. Game over on collision.
  3. Give both games a Control-based main menu with Play/Quit buttons using change_scene_to_file.
  4. Put HUD (score, moves) on a CanvasLayer so it stays fixed on screen.

title: Saving, Polish (Juice), Exporting, and the CAPSTONE
html:
Saving and loading

A portfolio game remembers things: high scores, settings, progress. Godot writes to a per-user folder accessed via the user:// path (the real location differs per OS, but user:// always works, including in web exports via browser storage). Use JSON for readable saves.

save_system.gd
extends Node const PATH := "user://save.json" func save_game(data: Dictionary) -> void: var f := FileAccess.open(PATH, FileAccess.WRITE) f.store_string(JSON.stringify(data)) f.close() func load_game() -> Dictionary: if not FileAccess.file_exists(PATH): return {} var f := FileAccess.open(PATH, FileAccess.READ) var text := f.get_as_text() f.close() var result = JSON.parse_string(text) return result if result is Dictionary else {}
Watch out.
Never save to res:// — that is your project, read-only once exported. Always use user:// for anything written at runtime.

Juice: the difference between a demo and a game

Juice is the cheap visual/audio feedback that makes a game feel alive. Add it last, add it everywhere:

  • Tweens for smooth motion: create_tween().tween_property(node, "scale", Vector2(1.2,1.2), 0.1) to pop a coin on pickup.
  • Screen shake: nudge the Camera2D offset randomly for a few frames on impact.
  • Particles: GPUParticles2D bursts for explosions and pickups.
  • Hit-stop: briefly set Engine.time_scale low on a big hit.
  • Sound on every action — even UI hovers.
juice.gd
func pop(node: Node2D) -> void: var t := create_tween() t.tween_property(node, "scale", Vector2(1.3, 1.3), 0.08) t.tween_property(node, "scale", Vector2.ONE, 0.08)

Exporting to desktop and web

Install export templates via Editor > Manage Export Templates. Then Project > Export, add a preset:

TargetNotes
Windows/macOS/LinuxProduces a native executable; set an icon and app name
Web (HTML5)Outputs index.html + data; runs in-browser, perfect for itch.io
Rule.
For web, use the Compatibility renderer (you chose it in Lesson 1) and test the export locally with a server (python -m http.server in the export folder) — opening index.html directly will fail due to browser security.
Tip.
Upload the web build's zipped folder to itch.io, tick "This file will be played in the browser," and you have a public, playable portfolio link to share with employers.

Choosing your capstone

Pick the game you love most from the arc — Breakout, Leap, Outpost, Last Stand, Shift, or Dash — and finish it properly. A portfolio game is judged on completeness and feel, not scope. One polished game beats six prototypes.

🎮 Build This: CAPSTONE — Ship One Game

This is the CAPSTONE: it extends ONE of the games you built earlier into a finished, portfolio-ready product across whatever genre you chose.

  1. Pick one prior game. Add a Control main menu (Play, Settings, Quit) and a game-over/win screen with restart.
  2. Add a settings menu that controls Music and SFX bus volumes.
  3. Implement save/load with the JSON system: persist the high score (and progress if relevant) to user://.
  4. Add juice: tweened pops, screen shake on impact, a particle burst, and sound on every meaningful action.
  5. Set the app icon and name, then export to desktop and to web; test the web build via a local server.
  6. Upload the web build to itch.io. You now have a shareable, finished Godot game and a multi-genre portfolio behind it. Congratulations.
